title: Sutra
subtitle: Cultural navigation for the Indian diaspora in Europe
excerpt: A mobile experience helping diaspora communities navigate identity, language, and belonging through calm, culturally-aware interaction patterns.
description: Sutra reimagines how second-generation immigrants interact with cultural resources — not as archives, but as living threads of connection.
researchInsight: Users don't want heritage delivered — they want to weave it into daily life.

challenge.headline: Cultural apps feel like museums. Diaspora life is not.
challenge.body: Second-generation Indians in Switzerland reported feeling caught between worlds — existing platforms either felt too traditional or too assimilated, with no space for the nuanced in-between.

research.summary: Eight weeks of cultural probes, diary studies, and community workshops in Zurich and Basel revealed that identity navigation is emotional, not informational.

findings.title: Language is emotional
findings.detail: Switching between Hindi and English carries social weight.

findings.title: Community over content
findings.detail: Users sought connection rituals, not content libraries.

findings.title: Privacy matters deeply
findings.detail: Cultural identity exploration requires safe, private spaces.

journeySteps.insight: Opens app during moments of cultural longing
Seeks shared experiences over expert content
Wants gentle daily rituals, not events

personas.name: Priya, 24
personas.quote: I don't want to choose between being Indian or Swiss. I want both, quietly.

personas.name: Arjun, 29
personas.quote: My Hindi is broken. The app shouldn't make me feel that.

phases.title: Cultural Probes
phases.description: Distributed probe kits to 12 participants across Zurich.

phases.title: Synthesis
phases.description: Affinity mapping surfaced three identity navigation modes.

phases.title: Co-Design
phases.description: Weekly workshops with diaspora community members.

phases.title: Validation
phases.description: Prototype testing with 20 participants over 2 weeks.

prototypes.title: Threads, not categories
prototypes.description: Cultural content organized as woven threads — users follow, combine, and create personal narratives.

interactions.name: Thread weaving
interactions.detail: Drag to connect cultural topics into personal journeys.

interactions.name: Gentle language switch
interactions.detail: Seamless bilingual transitions without mode switching.

interactions.name: Private reflection
interactions.detail: Personal notes visible only to the user.

lessons: Cultural UX requires community co-design, not expert assumptions.
Motion in cultural interfaces must feel warm, never performative.
Privacy is a design material — especially for identity exploration.
reflection.nextSteps: Exploring voice-based cultural storytelling and intergenerational connection features.

title: Transit Pulse
subtitle: Wayfinding research for Zürich's multilingual commuters
excerpt: Ethnographic research and interaction prototypes for ZVB — understanding how newcomers navigate transit in four languages.
description: A research-led project mapping confusion points in Zürich public transit for international residents and proposing interaction improvements.
researchInsight: Transit anxiety peaks at transfer moments, not at origin.

challenge.headline: Zürich transit is efficient. For newcomers, it doesn't feel that way.
challenge.body: International ZHdK students and recent arrivals reported high anxiety around transfers, ticket validation, and real-time disruption communication — despite ZVB's strong infrastructure.

research.summary: Ride-alongs, station intercepts, and journey diaries with 32 international commuters mapped emotional peaks across the transit experience.

findings.title: Transfer panic
findings.detail: Anxiety spikes during platform changes, not boarding.

findings.title: Ticket opacity
findings.detail: Zone systems remain confusing despite app availability.

findings.title: Disruption silence
findings.detail: Delay notifications lack actionable next steps.

personas.name: Mei, 22
personas.quote: I need to know I'm on the right platform before the train arrives.

personas.name: Carlos, 26
personas.quote: I've paid fines I didn't understand. The system assumes you know.

phases.title: Ethnography
phases.description: 32 ride-alongs across peak and off-peak hours.

phases.title: Journey Mapping
phases.description: Service blueprint across 8 touchpoints.

phases.title: Concept Design
phases.description: Three interaction concepts prototyped in Figma.

phases.title: Validation
phases.description: Wizard-of-Oz testing at Zürich HB.

prototypes.title: Calm at the transfer moment
prototypes.description: Three concepts focused on the transfer anxiety peak — ambient platform guidance, ticket clarity, and disruption recovery.

interactions.name: Platform pulse
interactions.detail: Ambient floor indicators showing correct boarding zone.

interactions.name: Ticket translator
interactions.detail: Plain-language ticket validation with visual confirmation.

interactions.name: Recovery paths
interactions.detail: When delayed, shows 2 clear alternatives instantly.

lessons: Service design research must include emotional mapping, not just task flows.
Multilingual interfaces need cultural context, not just translation.
The best transit UX disappears — it prevents anxiety before it forms.
reflection.nextSteps: Developing AR wayfinding prototype for Zürich HB transfer hall.

title: Echo Field
subtitle: Spatial audio interaction for museum exhibitions
excerpt: An immersive exhibition interface where visitors navigate soundscapes through gesture — developed for a contemporary art museum in Basel.
description: Echo Field transforms passive audio guides into spatial interaction — visitors shape their sonic environment through movement.
researchInsight: Sound creates presence. Navigation should follow the ear, not the eye.

challenge.headline: Museum audio guides narrate. They don't immerse.
challenge.body: Studio Forma's client wanted a digital layer that matched their exhibition's theme of sonic memory — existing audio guide patterns felt linear and institutional.

research.summary: Visitor observation, curator interviews, and sonic ethnography shaped a spatial interaction model for audio-first navigation.

findings.title: Ears lead in dark spaces
findings.detail: Visitors in dim galleries follow sound instinctively.

findings.title: Gesture feels natural
findings.detail: Hand movements to 'shape' sound tested positively.

personas.name: Curator Lena
personas.quote: Sound is the exhibition. The interface should conduct it.

personas.name: Visitor Tom
personas.quote: I want to wander and let the space guide me.

phases.title: Sonic Ethnography
phases.description: Mapped visitor sound behavior in 3 museums.

phases.title: Gesture Prototyping
phases.description: Leap Motion and Web Audio API experiments.

phases.title: Co-Design
phases.description: Weekly sessions with curatorial team.

phases.title: Exhibition
phases.description: Live deployment at Basel Art Week.

prototypes.title: Conduct your experience
prototypes.description: Visitors move hands to blend sound layers — proximity to artworks unlocks narrative fragments.

interactions.name: Sonic blend
interactions.detail: Gesture-controlled mixing of ambient and narrative layers.

interactions.name: Proximity unlock
interactions.detail: Approaching artworks reveals hidden audio fragments.

interactions.name: Memory capture
interactions.detail: Save sonic moments as shareable audio postcards.

lessons: Spatial interfaces need strong non-visual orientation cues.
Web Audio API performance is a design constraint — embrace it.
Curators are sonic directors — treat them as co-designers.
reflection.nextSteps: Exploring haptic feedback paired with spatial audio for accessibility.

title: Tactile Type
subtitle: Kinetic typography for low-vision readers
excerpt: An experiment in motion-enhanced typography — testing whether kinetic letterforms improve comprehension for low-vision digital readers.
description: Tactile Type explores the boundary between motion design and accessibility — when does animation help, and when does it harm?
researchInsight: Motion helps when it follows reading rhythm — not when it decorates.

challenge.headline: Accessibility tools prioritize function. Reading should still feel beautiful.
challenge.body: Low-vision users often rely on screen magnification and high contrast — but kinetic typography's potential for guided reading remains largely unexplored in digital contexts.

research.summary: Controlled studies with 18 participants tested 6 kinetic typography patterns at varying speeds and contrast levels.

findings.title: Contrast + motion compound
findings.detail: High contrast with subtle motion beats either alone.

findings.title: Personal preference varies
findings.detail: No single pattern works for all vision levels.

journeySteps.insight: Motion guides eye to word boundaries

personas.name: Helena, 45
personas.quote: Accessibility tools make me feel broken. I want design that respects me.

personas.name: Researcher Dr. Weiss
personas.quote: Show me the data. Then show me the beauty.

phases.title: Literature Review
phases.description: Survey of kinetic type and accessibility research.

phases.title: Pattern Design
phases.description: 6 motion patterns at 3 speed levels.

phases.title: User Testing
phases.description: Controlled comprehension tests with 18 participants.

phases.title: Synthesis
phases.description: Pattern library and guidelines published.

prototypes.title: Motion that reads with you
prototypes.description: Six kinetic patterns tested for comprehension, comfort, and preference across vision levels.

interactions.name: Reveal rhythm
interactions.detail: Letters appear in reading order at adjustable speed.

interactions.name: Personal preset
interactions.detail: Save motion preferences per user profile.

lessons: Accessibility research needs participants with lived experience, not simulations.
Motion timing matters more than motion style for comprehension.
Personal control is non-negotiable in accessible motion design.
reflection.nextSteps: Building open-source React component library for kinetic accessible typography.

title: Memory Loom
subtitle: Digital archive for oral family histories
excerpt: An interactive platform weaving oral histories into explorable narrative threads — born from documenting my grandmother's stories before they fade.
description: Memory Loom transforms linear oral history recordings into spatial, interconnected narratives families can explore together.
researchInsight: Memory is non-linear. Archives shouldn't be either.

challenge.headline: Family stories die in voice memos.
challenge.body: Oral histories remain trapped in linear recordings — unsearchable, unconnected, and increasingly lost as elders pass. My grandmother's stories sparked this project.

research.summary: Auto-ethnography, family interviews, and archival research informed a non-linear narrative interaction model.

findings.title: Stories connect laterally
findings.detail: One memory triggers another — not chronologically.

journeySteps.insight: Connects stories by theme, not date

personas.name: Grandmother Kamla
personas.quote: My stories are not data. They are gifts.

personas.name: Cousin Dev, 19
personas.quote: I never met my great-grandfather. I want to hear him.

phases.title: Auto-Ethnography
phases.description: Documented own family oral history practices.

phases.title: Platform Design
phases.description: Non-linear narrative architecture in React.

phases.title: Family Pilots
phases.description: Three families created living archives.

phases.title: Exhibition
phases.description: Interactive demo at ZHdK Year Show.

prototypes.title: Weave, don't list
prototypes.description: Stories exist as threads on a loom — users follow, branch, and connect narratives spatially.

interactions.name: Voice preservation
interactions.detail: Original audio always accessible alongside text.

interactions.name: Generational layers
interactions.detail: New family members add threads over time.

lessons: Personal projects carry the deepest research authenticity.
Non-linear navigation requires gentle orientation — families need guidance.
Voice is irreplaceable — never substitute with text alone.
reflection.nextSteps: Thesis development: scaling Memory Loom for diaspora communities beyond my own family.
